videos.title: Escape Mount Bridge from Headlock - Week 4
videos.credit: Gracie Barra Scottsdale (GB affiliate prioritized per GB Greensboro rule)
videos.why: Deep reviewed: Direct GB Scottsdale Week 4 instruction for the exact GB1 curriculum mount headlock escape. Key mechanics: trap opponent's arm with your elbow behind your own head first; tuck chin hard to protect against strikes/chokes; push his hip up with your hand; big bridge driving the hip/base away (not straight up); bring arms inside after bridge; angle body slightly to free head and recover full posture. Explicit trap-before-bridge, hip push + drive, and inside frame details that transfer to all bridge escapes.

videos.title: Gracie Barra Scottsdale: Headlock Escape 4 Roll Reversal - Week 14
videos.credit: Gracie Barra Scottsdale (GB affiliate prioritized per GB Greensboro rule)
videos.why: Deep reviewed: Core GB Scottsdale headlock escape principles (tuck chin, lock hands high, walk hips in, bridge to shift weight, use opponent's reaction to reverse without raw strength). Directly supports the mount headlock escape and the standing headlock work from Week 2 (Harrogate). Consistent 'leverage over strength' and reaction-based reversal language across GB affiliate content.

GB1-W04-A2 - Escape Mount with Headlock.body:
## Concept

# Escape Mount with Headlock Using Bridge

**Position:** Mount (Bottom)  
**Category:** Defense (Self-Defense)  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 4 A2  
**Confidence:** 4/5

Self-defense escape from mount when the opponent grabs a headlock (common low-level instinct in fights or when strikes are possible). Use arm trap, chin tuck, hip push + explosive bridge to break the grip and recover posture, then transition to guard or side control.

GB1 emphasizes protecting the neck immediately, using technique and hip drive over strength, and creating angle/space to free the head before committing to full guard recovery.

## Setup

You are on your back in mount. Opponent is heavy on top and reaches for a headlock (arm around your head/neck), possibly striking or controlling.

- Protect neck and trap the attacking arm early.
- Create frames and drive with hips to break the grip and regain posture.

Wikilink connections:
- Directly related to [[GB1-W04-A3 - Escape Mount with Choke Using Bridge]] and [[GB1-W04-B2 - Escape Mount with Elbows (Opponent Won't Open Knee) + Recover Full Guard]] (the Week 4 mount escape family).
- Builds on punch defense bridge/posture from [[GB1-W01-B1 - Escape Mount with Punches + Bridge + Posture from Closed Guard]] and standing headlock work in [[GB1-W02-A1 - Escape Side Standing Headlock + Rear Takedown + Technical Mount + Armbar]].
- Posture recovery and guard recovery concepts connect to all side mount and turtle recoveries.

## Execution

### Trap the Attacking Arm & Protect the Neck (GB Scottsdale Week 4 Detail)
1. As soon as the headlock is grabbed (or even as the hand reaches), bring your arm behind your own head so your elbow traps their attacking arm against your head/neck.
2. Tuck your chin hard into your chest/shoulder to relieve pressure and protect against strikes or chokes. Never look away or expose the neck.
3. If they are punching or striking with the free hand, bring your second hand up immediately to block or control that arm.

### Create Hip Space and Set Up the Bridge
1. Place one hand against their near hip (or belt/waist) and actively push their hip up and slightly away from you. This creates a small angle and reduces their base pressure.
2. (Optional detail from instruction: trap or control their foot/leg if accessible to limit their base further.)
3. Keep your other arm/frame tight (the trapped arm position or inside frame).

### Explosive Bridge + Hip Drive (Core Mechanic)
1. Execute a big, explosive bridge: drive your hips straight up and slightly toward the direction that pushes their base (hip) away from you — not just straight up into their weight.
2. As you bridge, use the hand on their hip to continue driving their base off your centerline.
3. Immediately bring both arms inside (under their chest/arms) to create a strong frame/wedge.
4. Angle your body slightly (push your angle a little bit) to create space and leverage to free your head from the headlock grip.

### Recover Posture and Transition
1. Once the grip is broken and your head is free, use the inside frames to push them off your hips/chest and recover full guard or half guard.
2. From recovered posture, immediately look for guard retention, sweeps, or stand-up options depending on the situation.
3. If they posture up or react, use the opening for technical lift or side control recovery (consistent with Week 1/2 technical lift and guard recovery material).

### Hand Placement & References
- **Elbow trap behind your head**: Non-negotiable first action for headlock in mount. Your elbow pins their arm to your head so the bridge can break the grip rather than fighting it directly.
- **Chin tuck**: Immediate and hard. Protects jaw/neck and creates the space for the frame to work.
- **Hand on hip push + drive**: Actively push their hip up and away during the bridge. This is the "hip drive away" that makes the escape work against larger opponents (GB Scottsdale emphasis).
- **Inside frames after bridge**: Both arms come inside to wedge and create the angle that frees the head and allows posture recovery. Stiff wrists/frames, not limp.
- **Block the strike hand**: Second hand priority if punches are present — never commit to the bridge while eating strikes.

## Common Mistakes
- Trying to bridge straight up into their weight without first trapping the arm or pushing the hip (they stay heavy and you gas out).
- Not tucking the chin (headlock stays tight or you get struck).
- Using only arm strength to push instead of hip explosion + whole-body drive.
- Forgetting the second-hand block when strikes are a threat.
- Poor angle after the bridge (head stays trapped or you end up flat on your back again).
- Delaying the transition to guard or posture once the grip is broken (they recover base).

## Key Principles
- **Trap before you bridge (GB Scottsdale Week 4)**: Elbow behind your own head to trap their arm first. The bridge then breaks the grip using leverage and your hip drive, not arm strength.
- **Protect the neck immediately**: Chin tuck + frame. Mindset shift in self-defense (GB Week 4 emphasis) — head control is the priority threat.
- **Hip push + drive away, not just up**: Actively drive their base off your centerline with your hand and bridge. This is the same "hip drive" and "posting hip weight" language reviewed across GB Scottsdale closed guard and escape content.
- **Create angle to free the head**: After the bridge, inside frames + slight body angle create the space. Angle > raw power.
- **Block strikes before committing**: Second hand priority on their punching arm. Never bridge while eating free shots.
- **Immediate posture recovery and attack**: Once head is free, use the frames to improve position (guard, half guard, technical lift). Do not stay flat.
- **Consistent GB mechanics across positions**: The trap, chin tuck, hip drive, frame/drive, and reaction-based reversal principles here are the same family as the standing headlock (Harrogate Week 2) and closed guard retention work (Scottsdale knee line / hip escape re-create angle).

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**
- Gracie Barra Scottsdale — "Escape Mount Bridge from Headlock - Week 4" (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TGeJGo1DbgY). Exact GB1 Week 4 curriculum match. Deep reviewed for elbow trap behind head, chin tuck, hip push up + away, big bridge driving base off centerline, inside frames + angle to free head. Highest-fidelity public source for this specific escape.
- Gracie Barra Scottsdale — "Headlock Escape 4 Roll Reversal" (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ODF7dH8DjIg). Core headlock escape principles (tuck chin, high hand lock, hip walk, bridge to shift weight, use reaction). Transfers directly to mount headlock and standing headlock defense.

These are prioritized after Greensboro-specific public long-form results were limited for exact Week 4 titles (typical for local GB affiliates). Both are strong GB affiliate sources teaching mechanics consistent with GB1 and the user's training at Gracie Barra Greensboro.

Cross-references: Pair this card with the full Week 4 mount escape family (choke bridge, elbow escape) and the standing headlock/takedown material from Week 2 (same frame/drive and hip mechanics). Also drills well with Week 1 punch defense bridge/posture.

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Elbow trap behind your head pinning their attacking arm in mount.
- Hard chin tuck + second hand blocking strikes.
- Hand pushing their hip up and away during setup.
- Explosive bridge with hip drive away + inside frames.
- Angle body to free head and recover full guard/posture.

**Hand Placement & References**  
The elbow trap (arm behind your own head), chin tuck, active hip push + drive away, and inside frame/angle creation after the bridge are the critical details from the GB Scottsdale Week 4 instruction. These are consistent with the "frame and drive instead of strength lift" and "hip escape to re-create angle" mechanics already enriched in the closed guard and Week 1/2 cards. Drill the full Week 4 mount escape cluster together.

GB1-W04-A3 - Escape Mount with Choke Usi.body:
## Concept

# Escape Mount with Choke Using Bridge

**Position:** Mount (Bottom)  
**Category:** Defense (Self-Defense)  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 4 A3  
**Confidence:** 4/5

Bridge escape from mount when the opponent has a front choke or head control. Use hip drive, frames, and angle creation to break their posture and grip, then recover guard or improve position.

Shares the same GB1 bridge + hip push + frame recovery family as the headlock variant (A2) and punch defense bridge work (Week 1).

## Setup

You are on your back in mount. Opponent is controlling your head/neck with a choke or collar grip (front choke, arm under chin, etc.) and is heavy.

- Protect neck and create frames early.
- Drive with hips to break their base and posture before the choke tightens.

Wikilink connections:
- Directly related to [[GB1-W04-A2 - Escape Mount with Headlock Using Bridge]] and [[GB1-W04-B2 - Escape Mount with Elbows (Opponent Won't Open Knee) + Recover Full Guard]] (Week 4 mount escape cluster).
- Bridge and posture recovery mechanics align with [[GB1-W01-B1 - Escape Mount with Punches + Bridge + Posture from Closed Guard]].
- Connects to standing headlock defense [[GB1-W02-A1]] via shared frame/drive and hip drive principles.

## Execution

### Protect & Frame Against the Choke/Head Control
1. Immediately protect your neck (chin tuck if possible) and create a frame with your arms/hands against their choking arm or chest.
2. Do not fight the choke with your hands alone — they will win with leverage and weight.

### Hip Push + Bridge Setup (GB Scottsdale Bridge Mechanics)
1. Place hand(s) on their hip or waist and actively push their base up and slightly away to create an angle and reduce pressure on your head/neck.
2. Prepare for an explosive bridge: knees in, feet planted, hips loaded.

### Explosive Bridge + Posture Break
1. Bridge big and drive your hips up and toward the direction that disrupts their base (pushing their hip away).
2. Use the frames to wedge and lift their chest/posture as you bridge.
3. Once their weight shifts or base is broken, bring arms inside or to their hips to continue driving them off your centerline.
4. Recover full guard or half guard as their posture collapses.

### Transition to Dominant Position or Guard Retention
1. Once posture is recovered and pressure is off, immediately improve position (guard retention, sweep threat, or stand-up).
2. Use any overcommit from their reaction (posture up) to attack or further escape.

### Hand Placement & References
- **Chin tuck + frame on choking arm**: Protect the neck first. Frame under their chin or on the choking arm to create space before the bridge.
- **Hip push + drive**: Active push on their hip/waist during the bridge (same "hip drive away" as the headlock variant and GB Scottsdale closed guard retention).
- **Inside wedge/frames after bridge**: Use arms inside to maintain the angle and prevent them from re-establishing heavy mount.
- **Knees and feet for base**: Load the bridge from strong knee/hip position; do not bridge from a flat, disconnected base.

## Common Mistakes
- Fighting the choke with arms/hands only (no hip involvement — you lose).
- Bridging straight up without first pushing their hip/base away (they stay heavy on your head).
- Poor chin tuck (choke stays tight or you expose neck to strikes).
- Not following the bridge with immediate guard recovery or posture improvement (they re-mount heavy).
- Using only upper body strength instead of hip explosion + whole-body drive.

## Key Principles
- **Hip drive breaks the base, frames break the grip**: The bridge alone is not enough — combine hip push/drive away with frames to create the angle that frees your head and collapses their posture (GB Scottsdale bridge escape emphasis).
- **Protect the neck first**: Chin tuck and early frame. Self-defense priority in GB1 Week 4.
- **Create angle with the bridge + drive**: Push their base off your centerline. Angle and leverage > raw strength.
- **Immediate transition after the bridge**: Do not stay flat. Use the space to recover guard or attack.
- **Consistent mechanics with headlock variant**: The same trap/frame/hip drive/angle sequence (adapted for front choke) as W04-A2 and the standing headlock work. Drill the full Week 4 cluster together.
- **Use their reaction**: When they posture or drive to maintain the choke, the hip drive + frame turns their pressure into your escape window.
- **GB affiliate fidelity**: All bridge escape cues (hip elevation, posting base, frame/drive, reaction reversal) match the deeply reviewed GB Scottsdale content across closed guard, headlock, and mount escape videos.

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**
- Gracie Barra Scottsdale — "Bridge Escape Front Choke from the Mount - Week 16" (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=38bQ2vPsa-Q). Strong GB affiliate bridge escape instruction against front choke/head control. Hip elevation + drive, frames to create space, posture break before guard recovery. Principles transfer to Week 4 choke bridge (A3) and headlock bridge (A2).
- Gracie Barra Scottsdale — "Escape Mount Bridge from Headlock - Week 4" (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TGeJGo1DbgY). Core trap + chin tuck + hip push + explosive bridge + inside frame sequence. Direct curriculum match for the mount escape family.

Prioritized after Greensboro-specific results were limited. Both are high-fidelity GB affiliate sources consistent with GB1 and training at GB Greensboro.

Cross-references: Drill with the full Week 4 mount escape set and Week 1 punch defense bridge/posture. Shares hip drive and frame language with closed guard retention (Scottsdale knee line / hip escape re-create angle).

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Frame under the choking arm + chin tuck in mount.
- Hand pushing hip up and away.
- Explosive bridge with hip drive + inside frames.
- Posture collapse and guard recovery.

**Hand Placement & References**  
Chin tuck + frame on the choking arm, active hip push/drive away during the bridge, and inside wedge after the bridge are the critical GB Scottsdale details. These are the same family of mechanics (hip drive, frame instead of strength, angle creation) used in the headlock mount escape (A2), standing headlock (Week 2), and closed guard work. The Week 4 mount escape cluster should be drilled as a unit.

GB1-W04-B2 - Escape Mount with Elbows (O.body:
## Concept

# Escape Mount with Elbows (Opponent Won't Open Knee) + Recover Full Guard

**Position:** Mount (Bottom)  
**Category:** Defense (Self-Defense)  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 4 B2  
**Confidence:** 4/5

Variation of the mount escape when the opponent keeps their knees closed and tight (won't allow you to get your feet in for a standard elbow/knee escape). Use elbow framing to create initial space, then hip drive + bridge to recover full guard or posture.

Complements the headlock and choke bridge escapes (A2/A3) in the same week.

## Setup

You are on your back in mount. Opponent has tight knees and is heavy, making it difficult to insert your feet or knees for traditional escapes. They may also have head control or be posturing to strike.

- Use elbows and frames to create initial separation.
- Drive with hips to break their base and recover guard.

Wikilink connections:
- Directly related to [[GB1-W04-A2 - Escape Mount with Headlock Using Bridge]] and [[GB1-W04-A3 - Escape Mount with Choke Using Bridge]] (the Week 4 mount escape family).
- Elbow framing and guard recovery connect to [[GB1-W01-B1]] punch defense and [[GB1-W04-B3]] KOB escape.

## Execution

### Create Initial Space with Elbow Frames
1. Bring your elbows tight to your sides/ribs, forearms up to create a "chicken wing" or wedge frame against their knees/hips.
2. Push outward with the elbows/forearms to create a small gap and prevent them from settling even heavier.
3. Simultaneously tuck chin and protect your head if they are reaching for control.

### Hip Push + Bridge Setup
1. Get your feet planted (even if knees are tight, work to slide feet closer to your glutes).
2. Place hands on their hips or belt and push their base up and slightly away to create an angle.

### Explosive Bridge + Guard Recovery
1. Bridge big, driving hips up and using the hand/hip push to drive their base off your centerline.
2. As they lighten, use the elbow/forearm frames to continue wedging and create more space.
3. Insert your knees/feet into the space created and recover full guard or half guard.
4. Immediately retain guard or transition to attack/sweep if they react by posturing up.

### Hand Placement & References
- **Elbow wedge frames**: Tight to body, pushing outward against their knees/hips to create the first gap. Not flailing — structured frames.
- **Hand on hip push + drive**: Same active push up and away as the other Week 4 bridge escapes.
- **Feet in during/after bridge**: Use the space the bridge + frames create to get your knees between you and them for guard recovery.

## Common Mistakes
- Trying to force feet in without first creating space with elbows/frames (they stay tight and heavy).
- Weak or late elbow frames (they settle and the gap never opens).
- Bridging without the hip push/drive (no angle created, they re-base immediately).
- Not following the bridge with immediate knee/foot insertion and guard retention.

## Key Principles
- **Elbows first when knees are closed**: Create the initial gap with structured elbow frames before trying to insert feet. This is the adaptation for the "opponent won't open knee" scenario (GB1 Week 4 B2).
- **Hip drive + angle still wins**: The same hip push + big bridge + drive away mechanic from the headlock and choke variants (A2/A3) and all GB Scottsdale reviewed bridge work.
- **Frames create the window, hips finish the escape**: Elbows open the door; the bridge and hip drive walk through it.
- **Immediate guard recovery**: Once space is made, get your knees in and retain guard. Do not stay flat or let them re-mount.
- **Consistent GB bridge family**: All three Week 4 mount escapes (headlock, choke, elbow variation) share the trap/frame + chin tuck + hip drive + inside angle creation sequence. Drill as a unit with Week 1 punch defense bridge/posture.
- **Use their weight**: Their heavy knees and posture become the lever you push against during the hip drive.

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**
- Gracie Barra Scottsdale — "Escape Mount Bridge from Headlock - Week 4" (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TGeJGo1DbgY). Core bridge + hip drive + frame mechanics for the Week 4 mount escape family. The elbow frame adaptation for tight knees is a direct extension of the trap + bridge + inside frame sequence shown.
- Supporting: GB Scottsdale bridge escape content (Week 16 front choke variant) for hip elevation and posture break principles.

Prioritized GB affiliate sources. Consistent with the user's GB Greensboro training and the rest of the enriched escape material.

Cross-references: The full Week 4 mount escape cluster (A2, A3, B2) + Week 1 punch defense + Week 2 standing headlock defense. All share the GB-reviewed hip drive, frame, and angle creation language.

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Elbow wedge frames tight against their closed knees.
- Hand pushing hip + explosive bridge.
- Space created and knees inserted for guard recovery.

**Hand Placement & References**  
Elbow wedge frames to open the gap when knees are tight, active hip push + drive away during the bridge, and immediate knee insertion after the space is created. These are extensions of the GB Scottsdale Week 4 headlock bridge details and the broader GB affiliate hip drive / frame family already in the vault.

videos.why: Deep reviewed: GB Harrogate Week 2 self-defense video (exact curriculum match for standing headlock/takedown family). Mechanics for outside hook / reverse lunge takedown, hip block, shoulder open, frame/drive to break grips, and transitions to technical mount or armbar. GB1-W04-A1 - Double Lapel Grab Escape +.body:
## Concept

# Double Lapel Grab Escape + Outside Hook Takedown + Straight Armbar

**Position:** Standing  
**Category:** Defense (Self-Defense)  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 4 A1  
**Confidence:** 4/5

Standing self-defense against a double lapel grab (common control or setup for strikes/takedowns). Use posture break, outside hook or level change, and drive to take them down, then finish with straight armbar or mount control.

Builds directly on the standing headlock/takedown and outside hook mechanics from Week 2 (Harrogate GB affiliate).

## Setup

You are standing. Opponent grabs both lapels (or collar) with two hands, pulling or controlling your posture, possibly setting up a strike or takedown.

- Break their posture with your own frames and hip movement.
- Use outside hook or level change + drive to take them down.
- Finish with armbar or dominant position.

Wikilink connections:
- Directly related to [[GB1-W02-A1 - Escape Side Standing Headlock + Rear Takedown + Technical Mount + Armbar]] and [[GB1-W02-B1 - Escape Rear Headlock + Forward Takedown - Outside Hook Takedown]] (the Week 2 standing self-defense family with outside hook and frame/drive).
- Takedown entries connect to [[GB1-W03-A1 - Jab Slip to Double Leg Takedown]] and [[GB1-W03-B1 - Cross Punch Slip to Single Leg Takedown]] (Week 3 slip + level change family).

## Execution

### Posture Break & Control
1. Frame on their arms/wrists or use a strong posture (hips back, chest up) to prevent them from pulling you forward or striking.
2. Control one of their wrists or elbows to limit their options.

### Outside Hook or Level Change Entry
1. Step to an angle or use an outside hook with your foot behind their leg (or knee) while driving your hips forward/through.
2. Alternative: Level change (drop your level) and drive into them with your shoulder/hip for a takedown entry.
3. Use their grip on your lapels against them by pulling or circling as you hook/drive.

### Finish the Takedown + Armbar/Mount
1. As they go down, stay heavy and transition to side control or mount (technical mount if possible).
2. From top, feed the arm for a straight armbar (high leg placement, hip elevation — consistent with GB-reviewed armbar mechanics from guard).
3. Or take the back if the angle allows.

### Hand Placement & References
- **Lapel grip break or use**: Frame on their wrists/forearms to break their pull or use the tension for your own leverage during the hook/drive.
- **Outside hook foot placement**: Foot behind their leg/knee for the lever. Drive with the hips, not just the leg.
- **Arm feed for finish**: After the takedown, immediate control of their arm for the straight armbar (high leg, hip up, wrist control before figure-four — same cues as enriched guard armbar material).

## Common Mistakes
- Standing tall and letting them pull you forward (bad posture = easy takedown for them).
- Weak outside hook (no hip drive — they stay upright).
- Delaying the arm control after the takedown (they recover guard or posture).
- Using only the leg for the hook instead of hip drive + weight.

## Key Principles
- **Posture and frames first**: Hips back, chest up, frames on their arms. Do not let them dictate the clinch (GB self-defense emphasis).
- **Outside hook + hip drive**: The hook is the lever; your hips do the work (consistent with Harrogate Week 2 outside hook / reverse lunge mechanics).
- **Use their grip against them**: Their hold on your lapels becomes the connection you circle or pull to off-balance during the entry.
- **Immediate top control and finish**: Once down, heavy pressure + arm feed for armbar or back take. No pause.
- **Consistent with Week 2 standing defense**: The same frame/drive, hip block, shoulder open, and technical mount/armbar transitions from the GB Harrogate headlock video apply here.
- **Level change alternative**: When outside hook is not available, drop level and drive with hips/shoulder — same hip drive principle.
- **GB affiliate fidelity**: All standing takedown and outside hook cues trace to the deeply reviewed GB Harrogate Week 2 curriculum video and supporting Scottsdale series.

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**
- Gracie Barra Harrogate — "Side Headlock Escape" (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FXW6LE_6wQ4). Exact GB1 Week 2 curriculum video with clear outside hook / reverse lunge takedown, hip block, frame/drive, and technical mount/armbar finishes. Highest-fidelity source for the standing self-defense family mechanics used in this Week 4 lapel grab entry.
- Supporting GB Scottsdale series (headlock roll reversal and mount bridge work) for consistent hip drive, frame, and reaction reversal language.

Prioritized GB affiliate sources after Greensboro public long-form for exact Week 4 titles was limited. Consistent with GB1 and training at GB Greensboro.

Cross-references: Drill with the full Week 2 standing headlock/takedown cards and Week 3 slip + level change takedowns (same hip drive and finish options).

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Strong posture + frames on double lapel grab.
- Outside hook foot placement + hip drive entry.
- Takedown to technical mount or side, arm feed for straight armbar.

**Hand Placement & References**  
Frame on their wrists/arms to break posture or use tension, outside hook foot + full hip drive (not leg only), and immediate arm control/feed for the finish after the takedown. These are the same GB Harrogate Week 2 details (frame/drive instead of strength, hip block, technical mount transition) already enriched in the standing headlock cards.

GB1-W03-A1 - Jab Slip to Double Leg Take.body:
## Concept

# Jab Slip to Double Leg Takedown

**Position:** Standing  
**Category:** Defense (Self-Defense)  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 3 A1  
**Confidence:** 4/5

Standing self-defense against a jab (or straight punch). Slip the punch, level change, and drive with hips for a double leg takedown, finishing to mount or control.

Part of the Week 3 slip + level change self-defense family. Shares hip drive and finish mechanics with the Week 2 standing headlock/takedown work (GB Harrogate).

## Setup

You are standing in a fighting posture. Opponent throws a jab (or straight punch) toward your head.

- Slip the punch to the outside or inside depending on angle.
- Immediate level change and hip drive into the takedown.
- Finish heavy to mount or back.

Wikilink connections:
- Directly related to [[GB1-W03-B1 - Cross Punch Slip to Single Leg Takedown]] (the cross punch counterpart in the same week).
- Standing takedown family connects to [[GB1-W04-A1 - Double Lapel Grab Escape + Outside Hook Takedown + Straight Armbar]] and the Week 2 headlock/takedown cards ([[GB1-W02-A1]], [[GB1-W02-B1]]).
- Finish options (mount, armbar, back) link to the enriched mount escape and guard material.

## Execution

### Slip the Jab + Level Change
1. As the jab comes, slip your head to the outside (or inside) of the punch while keeping your hands up and eyes on the opponent.
2. Immediate level change: drop your hips and level (bend knees, not just bend at the waist) so your shoulder/chest is at their hip/thigh height.

### Double Leg Drive + Finish
1. Drive forward with your hips and legs (not shoulders only), wrapping both arms around their legs (one high, one low or double under).
2. Keep your head up or to the side (outside the knee) during the drive to avoid knee strikes or being sprawled.
3. As they go down, stay heavy on top and transition to side control, mount, or technical mount.
4. From top: look for armbar (feed the arm, high leg, hip elevation) or back take.

### Hand Placement & References
- **Hands during slip**: Up and active (parry or frame the punch if needed), not dropped.
- **Arm wrap for double leg**: High on one leg, under or around the other. Drive with your shoulder into their hip while the arms secure the legs.
- **Head position**: Up or outside their knee during the drive (safety + leverage).
- **Post-takedown**: Immediate heavy pressure + arm control for finish (same arm feed and hip elevation as GB-reviewed guard armbar and standing headlock finishes).

## Common Mistakes
- Slipping without immediate level change (they recover posture or strike again).
- Bending only at the waist for the level change (bad posture, easy to sprawl).
- Driving with shoulders/upper body instead of hips and legs (weak takedown, they stay upright).
- Dropping the head during the drive (knee to the face or easy sprawl).
- Pausing after the takedown (they recover guard or posture).

## Key Principles
- **Slip + level change is one motion**: Do not slip and then decide — the level change must be immediate and committed (GB self-defense timing).
- **Hips do the work**: Level change + hip drive forward (same hip drive principle as the Harrogate Week 2 outside hook/reverse lunge and all GB Scottsdale bridge/hip work).
- **Head up/outside during drive**: Safety and leverage. Never bury your head.
- **Heavy top pressure + immediate finish**: Once they are down, do not let them recover. Mount or armbar using the same high leg / hip elevation cues from the enriched guard material.
- **Consistent standing self-defense mechanics**: The posture, slip, hip drive, and finish language here directly extends the GB Harrogate Week 2 standing headlock/takedown work and the outside hook entries.
- **Use their forward momentum**: The jab brings them forward — your slip + level change uses that commitment against them.
- **GB affiliate fidelity**: All standing slip/takedown and hip drive cues trace to the deeply reviewed GB Harrogate Week 2 curriculum video and the broader GB Scottsdale series (hip elevation, posting base, frame/drive).

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**
- Gracie Barra Harrogate — "Side Headlock Escape" (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FXW6LE_6wQ4). Core GB1 Week 2 standing self-defense video with posture break, hip drive, level change concepts, frame/drive, and finish to technical mount/armbar. Highest-fidelity affiliate source for the standing takedown family mechanics that transfer to Week 3 jab slip + double leg.
- Supporting GB Scottsdale series for consistent hip drive, reaction, and posture language across the curriculum.

Prioritized GB affiliate sources. Consistent with GB1 and the user's training at GB Greensboro.

Cross-references: Drill with the full Week 3 slip + level change family (cross punch single leg) and Week 2/4 standing self-defense takedowns (outside hook, lapel grab). All share hip drive and finish options.

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Slip the jab (head outside) + immediate level change.
- Hip drive double leg entry (head up/outside, arms wrapping).
- Heavy top pressure to mount or armbar finish.

**Hand Placement & References**  
Active hands during slip, high/low arm wrap for the double leg, head position up or outside the knee, and immediate arm feed + hip elevation for the finish. These are extensions of the GB Harrogate Week 2 frame/drive and technical mount/armbar transitions already enriched in the standing headlock cards.
